original_hypothesis.title: Bribe reward token set can be spammed with many connector tokens causing getReward to exceed gas limits and brick reward claiming
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Bribe.notifyRewardAmount() allows any caller to add a new rewards token if tokenHandler marks it as a connector (or it is already a bribe token). Each new token is pushed into bribeTokens. Later, gaugeManager-driven reward claiming may pass large token arrays (or iterate over bribeTokens off-chain), and each token triggers earned() which itself loops over epochs. A spammer can deposit dust amounts across many connector tokens to inflate bribeTokens length, making reward claiming prohibitively expensive or reverting due to gas limits.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Even with trusted sequencer/no mempool MEV, a griefing attacker can act entirely on-chain by paying small amounts to add reward tokens. This threatens availability of bribe claiming and can trap rewards in the bribe contract.
deployment_assumptions: TokenHandler governance whitelists many connector tokens or allows connector expansion over time
GaugeManager/getReward call path uses large tokens arrays (e.g., passes all bribeTokens) rather than a curated subset
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Bribe._isRewardToken accepts tokenHandler.isConnector(_rewardToken); connector set is governance-controlled in TokenHandler.sol but no limits are enforced in Bribe.sol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unbounded growth of reward token list via public function

preconditions: Attacker can source many connector tokens and call notifyRewardAmount with small rewards
TokenHandler marks those tokens as connectors (or tokens are otherwise accepted by _isRewardToken)
Reward claiming path attempts to claim across many tokens

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unbounded list growth and expensive per-token reward computation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: gas exhaustion for getReward loops in downstream callers
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cap on bribeTokens; no minimum reward threshold; no pagination in getReward itself

analysis_questions: How does GaugeManager build the `tokens` array passed to Bribe.getReward() in production?
Is there any on-chain mechanism to prune/blacklist reward tokens per bribe?
What is the worst-case gas for earned() * N tokens with WEEK=1800?
validation_requirements: Bench gas: earned() for varying epoch gaps; multiply by token count
Simulate spam adding 100+ reward tokens and claiming
Confirm whether core distribution relies on claiming 'all tokens' in one transaction

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly calls notifyRewardAmount for many connector tokens with minimal amounts, causing bribeTokens to grow. Claiming bribes for a tokenId becomes too expensive or reverts, effectively bricking bribe reward distribution for users.
prerequisites: Connector token set includes many tokens; attacker holds dust amounts

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Bribe rewards become unclaimable for affected bribes/epochs, trapping incentives
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All voters for pools with affected bribe contracts
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Limited; would require admin migration to new bribe contract or token pruning feature

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing stress tests for token list growth and claim gas

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor bribeTokens length growth per bribe

validation_result.mechanism: Denial of Service via array inflation and nested loops
validation_result.impact_summary: Attackers can inflate the `bribeTokens` array with dust amounts of connector tokens, causing reward claiming and frontend APIs to exceed gas limits due to expensive nested loops.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `Bribe.notifyRewardAmount()` function allows any user to add a reward token to the `bribeTokens` array, provided the token is whitelisted as a connector by the `TokenHandler`. There is no minimum reward amount required, meaning an attacker can deposit 1 wei of every available connector token to inflate the `bribeTokens` array for any given gauge.

When rewards are queried or claimed, the system iterates over these tokens. For example, `veNFTAPI._addExternalBribeRewards()` loops through all `bribeTokens` and calls `earned()` for each. The `earned()` function itself contains a loop over all epochs since the user's `lastEarn` timestamp. Because `WEEK` is set to 1800 seconds (30 minutes), the number of epochs grows very rapidly (48 per day). Inside this epoch loop, `earned()` performs two binary searches (`getPriorBalanceIndex` and `getPriorSupplyIndex`).

The time complexity to query or claim rewards becomes `O(T * E * log C)`, where `T` is the number of bribe tokens, `E` is the number of elapsed epochs, and `C` is the number of checkpoints. By maximizing `T` (adding all connector tokens), an attacker can easily cause these view functions and claiming transactions to exceed the block gas limit, effectively bricking the frontend API and trapping user rewards.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence clearly shows that `notifyRewardAmount` pushes to `bribeTokens` without any minimum threshold, relying only on `_isRewardToken` which accepts any connector. The `earned` function contains the `O(epochs)` loop with nested binary searches. `veNFTAPI` demonstrates the exact vulnerable pattern of iterating over all `bribeTokens` and calling `earned`.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Allows any caller to push a new token to the `bribeTokens` array as long as it is a connector, with no minimum reward amount to prevent spam.
Iterates over every epoch since the last claim. With WEEK=1800, epochs accumulate rapidly. Inside the loop, it performs expensive binary searches.
Iterates over all tokens in `bribeTokens` and calls the expensive `earned()` function, leading to out-of-gas reverts when `bribeTokens` is spammed.

calling_sequence: Bribes pushes connectorToken to bribeTokens array
veNFTAPI calls Bribes.earned() for each token
Bribes.earned() loops over all epochs and performs binary searches
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attacker repeatedly calls `notifyRewardAmount` with different connector tokens and dust amounts. The `bribeTokens` array grows. When `veNFTAPI.getAllPairRewards` is called, it loops over `external_bribes_input.tokens` (which is `bribeTokens.length`). For each token, it calls `earned()`, which loops over `numEpochs` and calls `getPriorBalanceIndex` and `getPriorSupplyIndex`. The nested loops and binary searches consume excessive gas, causing the transaction to revert.

deployment_assumptions_checked: TokenHandler governance whitelists many connector tokens or allows connector expansion over time
GaugeManager/getReward call path uses large tokens arrays (e.g., passes all bribeTokens) rather than a curated subset
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The provided deployment scripts do not show the exact number of whitelisted connectors or the implementation of GaugeManager. However, the presence of `veNFTAPI` in scope confirms the vulnerability affects the frontend API regardless of GaugeManager's implementation.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker identifies all whitelisted connector tokens in the `TokenHandler`. They call `notifyRewardAmount` on a target gauge's external bribe contract, depositing 1 wei of each connector token. This inflates the `bribeTokens` array. When users attempt to load their rewards via `veNFTAPI.getAllPairRewards`, the RPC call runs out of gas and fails, breaking the UI. If the protocol's claiming mechanism also iterates over all `bribeTokens`, users will be unable to claim their legitimate rewards.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High. The attack is extremely cheap (dust amounts of tokens) and can completely break the frontend's ability to display rewards, as well as potentially bricking the on-chain claiming process if it relies on iterating the full token list.
recommended_mitigations: Implement a minimum reward amount threshold in `notifyRewardAmount` to make spamming economically unviable.
Limit the maximum length of the `bribeTokens` array per gauge.
Optimize the `earned()` function by caching the binary search results or restructuring the checkpoint logic to avoid O(epochs) iteration.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy the protocol contracts including TokenHandler, Bribes, and veNFTAPI. 2. Whitelist 20 different ERC20 tokens as connectors in TokenHandler. 3. Call `notifyRewardAmount` on a Bribe contract with 1 wei for each of the 20 connectors. 4. Advance time by 10 days (480 epochs). 5. Call `veNFTAPI.getAllPairRewards` for a user who has a deposit in the gauge. 6. Observe the call reverting due to out-of-gas.
uncertainties: The exact implementation of `GaugeManager.getReward` is unknown, so it is unclear if on-chain claiming is completely bricked or if users can bypass the DoS by claiming tokens individually.
additional_testing_needed: Benchmarking the gas cost of `earned()` with varying numbers of epochs and checkpoints.
Testing the `GaugeManager` claiming flow to see if it forces iteration over all `bribeTokens`.
